figures.gloss: The single God above all, named first in every prayer at the kuvd feast but owning no shrine of his own — governance of the lived world is delegated to the dzuar powers beneath him, whom he sends as a lord sends stewards.
figures.facet: A monotheistic apex over a working polytheism: the structure lets Assianism argue with a straight face that it was never pagan — one God, many commissioned powers.
figures.provenance: Ossetian kuvd prayer formulae recorded in 19th-20th-c. ethnography; V. I. Abaev's Ossetic lexicon (Khuytsau); Assianist statements of doctrine (post-Soviet).

figures.gloss: The chief dzuar — patron of men, travelers, warriors, and oaths, imagined as a white-bearded rider on a white horse. Women traditionally may not speak his name, calling him 'laegty dzuar', the men's dzuar; his week-long November feast Dzheorguyba is the biggest festival of the Ossetian year.
figures.facet: His name is St. George worn down by a millennium of Ossetic speech (Uas Gergi, 'Saint George'), and Dzheorguyba tracks the Georgian Giorgoba — yet the figure himself kept the rider-with-lance frame and dropped the church: the saint fully re-grown into a native god.
figures.provenance: Abaev's etymology of Uastyrdzhi; 19th-20th-c. Ossetian ethnography of the Dzheorguyba cycle; the living cult at Nykhas Uastyrdzhi and other shrines.

figures.gloss: The oral epic of the Nart heroes — Soslan tempered in the forge, Batraz the steel-bodied, Satana the matriarch, and the Nartamongae, the wondrous cup that rises of itself to the hero whose boast is true. Recorded from the 1860s-70s onward, it is the mythological backbone Assianism reads as its scripture-equivalent.
figures.facet: Dumezil built much of comparative Indo-European mythology on this corpus, arguing it preserves Scythian epic memory carried into the mountains by the Alans — an oral text functioning as a two-and-a-half-millennium archive.
figures.provenance: Ossetian Nart collections from the Shanaev brothers (1870s) onward; Georges Dumezil, 'Legendes sur les Nartes' (1930) and 'Le livre des heros' (1965); V. I. Abaev's Nart studies.

figures.gloss: At every kuvd the elder prays over three round cheese pies stacked with the sacrificial meat and a drink — three at feasts, an even two at memorials for the dead, the count itself liturgical. The customary gloss reads the three as God, the Sun, and the Earth: the cosmos set out on the table before the first toast.
figures.facet: A complete vertical cosmology encoded in festive food — the rite survives every overlay precisely because it looks like dinner.
figures.provenance: Living Ossetian practice, documented in 19th-20th-c. ethnography and standardized in Assianist observance; the three-vs-two pie distinction is a widely recorded funerary rule.

figures.gloss: A freestanding round wood on the Alagir plain, sacred and untouchable: in the legend the fleeing Khetag could not reach the forest, so the forest tore itself loose and flew to shelter him. No branch may be carried out; the Day of Khetag was revived as an all-Ossetian festival in post-Soviet North Ossetia.
figures.facet: The genius loci inverted — instead of a spirit dwelling in a place, the place itself acts, moving to save a man; the grove's sanctity is remembered as its own deed.
figures.provenance: The Khetag legend (also reworked by Kosta Khetagurov's poem 'Khetag'); the living grove and festival near Suadag, Alagir district.

figures.gloss: One word for the holy place, the power resident at it, and the 'saint' addressed there — hilltop enclosures, sacred trees, and stone sanctuaries like Rekom, each with its feast day, its animal offering, and its beer. The word itself is borrowed from Georgian jvari, 'cross'.
figures.facet: An entire ecclesiology compressed into a loanword: the vocabulary is Christian Georgian, the institution it names is the native mountain shrine — the seam between host church and folk religion visible inside a single term.
figures.provenance: V. I. Abaev, 'Historical-Etymological Dictionary of Ossetic' (dzuar < Georgian jvari); ethnography of the Rekom and other North Ossetian shrine cults.

figures.gloss: The forge-tempered Nart hero, born from a stone struck by desire for Satana's beauty and hardened in the celestial smith Kurdalagon's forge — invulnerable everywhere except the knees, where the tongs gripped him during tempering. He is slain when the trickster Syrdon tricks him into exposing that one weak point to the rolling Wheel of Balsag.
figures.facet: His invulnerability is manufactured, not innate, and it is manufacture that leaves the flaw: the tongs that make him unbeatable are also what mark the one place he can still be reached, binding his heroism permanently to the smith who forged him.
figures.provenance: Ossetian Nart sagas recorded from the Shanaev brothers (1870s); Georges Dumezil, 'Légendes sur les Nartes' (1930) and 'Le livre des héros' (1965); V.I. Abaev's Nart studies.

figures.gloss: The matriarch of the Ossetian Narts — hostess, counselor, and contriver whose wit repeatedly rescues the war-band from famine, siege, and its own quarrels. In the stone-birth episode common to the Ossetian and neighboring Circassian tellings, she is named as Soslan's mother, born of a herdsman's desire kindled by the sight of her bathing.
figures.facet: Her power runs entirely through domestic mastery and cunning speech rather than arms: Satana never fights, yet no Nart campaign succeeds without her foresight, making household competence — not combat — the sagas' other axis of heroism.
figures.provenance: Ossetian Nart collections from the Shanaev brothers (1870s) onward; Georges Dumezil's Nart studies, which treat Satana as the corpus's central sovereignty-and-hospitality figure.

figures.gloss: The steel-bodied storm-hero of the Nart sagas, tempered whole in Kurdalagon's heavenly forge and rendered invulnerable, whose rages summon storm and slaughter alike; he cannot die until, at his own dying command, his enormous sword is finally cast into the sea.
figures.facet: Dumezil made Batraz's death-scene the centerpiece of a Eurasia-wide comparison: a hero's power is sealed inside a weapon that must be returned to water before he can die, the same closing rite of the sword-and-the-lake found far outside the Caucasus.
figures.provenance: Ossetian Nart sagas (Shanaev brothers, 1870s); Georges Dumezil, 'Légendes sur les Nartes' (1930), which sets Batraz's sword-in-the-sea scene beside the wider Indo-European sword-return motif.

figures.gloss: The malicious wit of the Narts, born of the water-spirit Donbettyr's daughter, who provokes quarrels, exposes secrets, and engineers heroes' downfalls with mocking songs; his grief at the murder of his sons, killed in a Nart blood-feud he himself helped provoke, drives him to make the first fandyr harp from their sinews.
figures.facet: Grief here is converted directly into art: the instrument that will carry the Narts' songs forever after is built from the very catastrophe the trickster's own mischief set in motion — culture's founding sound cut from the trickster's private ruin.
figures.provenance: Ossetian Nart sagas (Shanaev brothers, 1870s); Georges Dumezil, who treats Syrdon as the explicit Ossetian counterpart to the Norse Loki within his comparative Indo-European framework.

figures.gloss: The celestial smith of the Nart sagas, who forges and tempers the great heroes — Soslan and Batraz among them — along with their weapons and gear in his sky-forge, marking each hero's body with the one flaw the tempering process leaves behind.
figures.facet: Divine craftsmanship here is inseparable from vulnerability: Kurdalagon does not simply arm his heroes, he manufactures their single point of failure as the necessary residue of making them otherwise unbeatable.
figures.provenance: Ossetian Nart sagas (Shanaev brothers, 1870s); V.I. Abaev's Nart studies; discussed comparatively by Dumezil alongside the Circassian smith-god Tlepsh.

figures.gloss: Lord of thunder, rain, and the grain harvest, among the most-worshipped powers of the Ossetian shrine cult, grown from the Caucasian cult of St. Elijah (Uac-Illa, 'Holy Elijah'); places struck by lightning fall under his rite, and his summer feast petitions him for a good harvest.
figures.facet: A second, quieter St.-saint seam beside Uastyrdzhi/St. George: where the warrior-patron kept the rider-with-lance frame and dropped the church, Uacilla shows the same re-paganizing process worked on a different imported saint, this time fused to a thunder-and-harvest office the saint himself never had.
figures.provenance: V.I. Abaev's etymology of Uac-Illa; 19th-20th-c. Ossetian ethnography of the shrine-cult calendar.

figures.gloss: Lord of the wild beasts and master of the hunt, richly attested in Ossetian hunting songs — one-eyed in some tellings, he decides which hunter receives a kill and which returns empty-handed; hunters owed him the first portion of any game and sang his praise-songs before setting out.
figures.facet: The hunt here is a transaction with an owner, not a taking from an ownerless wild: every kill is understood as Afsati's gift dispensed on terms, making the hunter a supplicant to the animal's true keeper rather than its conqueror.
figures.provenance: Ossetian hunting-song ethnography recorded in the 19th-20th centuries; V.I. Abaev's Nart and folklore studies.

figures.gloss: Sovereign of the Ossetian land of the dead, who receives and assigns the newly dead their places in the otherworld; the funeral horse-dedication rite (baekhfaeldisyn), in which a rider's horse is ritually consecrated and sent after its master, was understood to speed the dead to his realm.
figures.facet: Governance of the dead here is bureaucratic rather than judicial: Barastyr assigns place, he does not weigh deeds — the moral reckoning that in many afterlife systems belongs to the underworld sovereign is absent, leaving him closer to an administrator than a judge.
figures.provenance: Ossetian funerary ethnography (19th-20th c.) documenting the baekhfaeldisyn horse-dedication rite; V.I. Abaev's lexicon and folklore studies.

figures.gloss: Deity of the supra-hearth chain (rekhy), the holiest object of the traditional Ossetian house, suspended over the hearth-fire and treated as a shrine in itself; oaths were sworn by the chain, and a bride was formally presented to it on entering her husband's home.
figures.facet: The household's whole sacred order is condensed into a single hanging object rather than a room or a rite: Safa has no temple, no feast day of his own scale to match Uastyrdzhi or Uacilla — his cult is exactly as large as one iron chain, and no larger.
figures.provenance: Ossetian domestic-ethnography accounts of the rekhy hearth-chain cult, 19th-20th c.

edges.note: One epos, two peoples: Circassian Sosruko is the same Nart hero as Ossetian Soslan/Sosryko — the shared North Caucasus Nart corpus is documented, corpus-level common inheritance.
Satanaya is Satana of the Ossetian Nart cycle — the same matriarch presiding over both tellings of the shared epic.
Neighboring North Caucasus grove-sanctities — worship under living trees instead of a built temple; areal kinship between adjacent peoples, no derivation shown.

THE LIVING DESCENDANT SEAM: Ossetic is the proven linguistic descendant of Alanic, the surviving Scytho-Sarmatian tongue; Dumezil and Abaev read the Nart corpus as that lineage's epic memory carried off the steppe into the Caucasus.

Dumezil's showpiece correspondence: the Nartamongae, the cup that rises to the truthful hero, continues the Scythians' heaven-sent golden cup that only the worthy king could take — the same sovereignty-test vessel, twenty-five centuries apart.
Intra-tradition architecture: the Great God holds no shrine and the shrines hold no Great God — Khuytsau delegates, the dzuars administer.
The rite is the theology performed: the elder's prayer over the three pies addresses Styr Khuytsau first, then the dzuars in order — the delegation hierarchy spoken aloud at every feast.
Both continue the medieval Caucasian cult of St. George (Uas Gergi / Giorgi) re-grown into the chief native divine patron — one saint, two neighboring re-paganizations, the names still cognate.

Documented transmission in a word: Ossetic dzuar is the Georgian jvari ('cross') borrowed across the range — and on both slopes the 'cross' names a shrine-being with feast, beer, and sacrifice, not a church.
The grove's shrine is held to be Uastyrdzhi's — the acting place and the patron of travelers joined in one sanctuary where Khetag was saved mid-flight.

Adjacent mountain shrine institutions — height sanctuaries, feast-day pilgrimage, sacrifice and beer, and a god-word fused with a shrine-word (yerda / dzuar); strong areal kinship between next-door neighbors, left at structural tier.
The same forge-tempered, invulnerable-but-for-one-point Nart hero under his Ossetian and Circassian names — proven shared inheritance of a single North Caucasus epic figure.
The same Nart matriarch-hostess under her Ossetian and Circassian names — one figure, two tellings of the shared corpus.

Dumezil's explicit structural comparandum within comparative Indo-European mythology: two malicious tricksters whose mischief provokes the deaths of their own community's great heroes — no shared origin claimed, a scholarly structural parallel.

The Ossetian and Circassian celestial smiths of the shared Nart-cycle tradition, both forging and tempering the epic heroes and their gear — an attested comparative pairing in Dumezil's Nart scholarship, though the names are not linguistically cognate.

Two independent pre-Christian thunder gods whose storm-and-harvest functions were absorbed into the cult of St. Elijah after Christianization — Ossetian Uacilla (Uac-Illa) and Slavic Perun, whose power migrated to Ilya Prorok in East Slavic folk religion; parallel process, no direct contact.

Two master-of-the-hunt figures who decide the outcome of every kill and are owed ritual return by the hunter — structural analogy between an Ossetian shrine-deity and a Greek Olympian, no descent claimed.

A loose, purely structural reach: both are sovereigns who receive and administer the dead in a fixed otherworld realm, but with no equivalent judicial or agricultural entanglements shared between them — flagged as the weakest of this file's cross-tradition seams.
Intra-tradition bond: Kurdalagon tempers Soslan in the sky-forge, the act that both makes and marks him.
Intra-tradition bond: the same celestial smith tempers Batraz's steel body as he does Soslan's.
Intra-tradition bond: Satana is named as Soslan's mother in the stone-birth episode, making the sagas' central matriarch and central invulnerable hero a documented parent-child pair.
Intra-tradition bond: Syrdon's mockery is the documented mechanism that exposes Soslan's one vulnerable point to the rolling Wheel of Balsag — the trickster engineering the invulnerable hero's death.
Intra-tradition bond: the same delegation architecture already established for Uastyrdzhi and the three-pies rite — the Great God delegates governance of the dead to Barastyr as steward.
Intra-tradition bond: Uacilla holds the thunder-and-harvest office as one of the Great God's commissioned dzuar powers.
Intra-tradition bond: Afsati holds the hunt as one of the Great God's commissioned dzuar powers, the same delegated-governance structure as Uacilla and Barastyr.

Intra-tradition bond: the even two-pie count offered at memorial feasts is food set out for the dead on their way to Barastyr's realm, distinct from the three-pie count addressed to God, Sun, and Earth at ordinary kuvd feasts.
The same pan-Caucasian Nart epic tradition in its Chechen-Ingush and Ossetian branches — proven shared descent, not mere analogy, per the comparative scholarship (Dumezil and successors).
an oral epic-cycle functioning as a tradition's scripture-equivalent, preserving cosmology and heroic genealogy in sung/chanted verse.

dossier.essence: The folk religion of the Ossetes — the Iranian-speaking descendants of the medieval Alans in the central Caucasus — organized around one Great God, Styr Khuytsau, and the dzuar shrine-powers who administer the world for him. Carried continuously as shrine-and-feast practice beneath Christian and Soviet overlays, it was codified after 1991 as organized Uatsdin (Assianism) and is today a visible public religion in North Ossetia.
dossier.cosmos: Styr Khuytsau owns no shrine and delegates: Uastyrdzhi — St. George's name worn down into a native god — patrols roads, men, and oaths, and each dzuar is at once holy place, resident power, and the 'saint' addressed there. The customary reading of the three ritual pies sets out the cosmos on the feast table as God, Sun, and Earth. The Nart sagas supply the mythic past, and the dead remain participants: at memorial feasts the pie-count drops from three to two, and the year's commemorations feed and honor them.
dossier.practice: The kuvd feast is the central institution: the eldest man prays over three stacked cheese pies, sacrificial meat, and drink, naming Styr Khuytsau first and the dzuars in order; no professional clergy intervenes. Shrine festivals structure the year — above all the week-long Dzheorguyba for Uastyrdzhi in November — with pilgrimage to sanctuaries like Rekom and the revived all-Ossetian Day of Khetag at Khetag's Grove. Post-Soviet Assianist organizations have standardized calendar and observance without closing the tradition into a church.
dossier.canon: Oral: the Ossetian Nart corpus (recorded from the 1860s-70s onward; studied by Dumézil, translated in Colarusso and Salbiev 2016) and the kuvd prayer formulae; organized Assianism has produced doctrinal statements but no closed scripture.
dossier.reflects: Religious continuity as archive — a feast-and-shrine practice that carried Scytho-Sarmatian epic memory and a working folk ecclesiology intact beneath a millennium of official religions.
sources: John Colarusso & Tamirlan Salbiev (eds.), 'Tales of the Narts: Ancient Myths and Legends of the Ossetians' (2016)
Richard Foltz, 'Scythian Neo-Paganism in the Caucasus: The Ossetian Uatsdin as a Nature Religion' (2020)
V. I. Abaev, 'Istoriko-etimologicheskiy slovar osetinskogo yazyka' (1958-1989)

links.cite: Richard Foltz, 'Scythian Neo-Paganism in the Caucasus: The Ossetian Uatsdin as a Nature Religion' (2020)
John Colarusso & Tamirlan Salbiev (eds.), 'Tales of the Narts: Ancient Myths and Legends of the Ossetians' (2016)
